text: Five laparoscopic ports are visible, and the surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to mobilize the colon, detaching it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon, effectively mobilizing a section of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon gradually mobilizes the colon, dividing the peritoneum on the left side to mobilize the rectum. Care is taken to avoid injuring the ureter during this dissection. A similar dissection is performed on the right side of the patient.
The surgeon performs dissection in the presacral fascia, carefully separating the mesorectum and incorporating it with the specimen.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform the dissection, continuing the mobilization of the colon and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon utilizes a monopolar hook cautery to perform dissection, demonstrating an alternative technique for tissue separation in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects and divides the lateral ligaments of the sigmoid colon using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection, advancing further down into the pelvis, with visible separation of tissue layers as the procedure progresses.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric vein, applies hemolog clips to secure it, and then divides the vein, completing this step of the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the sacral fascia, progressing further caudally within the pelvic region.
The surgeon applies traction to the colon, providing a clear view of the surrounding area for further dissection.
The surgeon further dissects and divides the lateral ligaments on both sides using a harmonic scalpel, continuing the dissection into the pelvic region.
The surgeon uses a bowel grasper to pull the rectum cranially, facilitating further dissection within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to further divide the lateral ligaments, continuing the dissection on both sides of the rectum.
The surgeon performs dissection between the bladder and the rectum, carefully separating these two structures.
The surgeon encounters the seminal vesicles during the dissection, ensuring their preservation as the procedure progresses.
The surgeon clips the inferior mesenteric artery using hemolog clips and then divides it, completing the vascular control step in the procedure.
The surgeon observes the pulsations of the inferior mesenteric artery, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the caudal dissection, advancing deeper into the pelvic cavity, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon divides the mesocolon in the area between the bladder and rectum, following previous dissection steps.
The surgeon approaches the area of colonic transection, preparing to divide the colon at this specific site.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect and divide the mesocolon, continuing the surgical procedure in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses an Endo GIA stapler to transect the colon, completing the colonic transaction in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the procedure by creating a permanent colostomy from the descending colon. The specimen is extracted through the perineal dissection, marking the completion of the surgery.
